title: Projects, Wikis & Discussions
description: GitHub Projects, Wikis, and Discussions extend collaboration beyond code — tracking work, documenting knowledge, and fostering community.

sections.title: GitHub Projects
sections.description:
Projects (v2) are spreadsheet-like kanban boards powered by GitHub's GraphQL API. Use custom fields (text, number, date, single/multi-select), iteration cycles, and views (board, table, timeline). Automate status by linking to issues and PRs — drag items between columns or use workflow rules.

gh project create --org my-org --title "Sprint 24"
gh project close 
# View board
https://github.com/orgs/my-org/projects/1

sections.title: Wiki Features
sections.description:
Every GitHub repo has a wiki — a separate Git repo for documentation. Wikis support Markdown, reStructuredText, Textile, and AsciiDoc. Sidebar (_Sidebar.md) and footer (_Footer.md) are customizable. Wikis can be cloned and edited locally.

# Clone the wiki repo
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.wiki.git
# Wiki structure
Home.md
Getting-Started.md
_Sidebar.md
_Footer.md

sections.title: Discussions
sections.description:
Discussions provide a forum-style space for Q&A, ideas, show-and-tell, and polls. Categories organize topics (Announcements, General, Ideas, Q&A, Show and Tell). Comments can be upvoted, and answers can be marked as solutions. Discussions are searchable and linkable from issues.

# Discussion categories
💡 Ideas — feature suggestions
🙋 Q&A — help and questions
📣 Show and Tell — demos
📢 Announcements — maintainer posts

sections.title: Cross-Linking
sections.description:
Issue-to-PR cross-references (#123) create timeline events. Project items auto-link to issues and PRs. Discussions can be converted to issues. Wikis can link to issues and commits. The dependency graph tracks libraries.

# Autolink references
org/repo#123 — cross-repo issue
user/repo@abc123 — cross-repo commit
GH-123 — custom autolink

sections.title: Automation Rules
sections.description:
Project workflows can auto-move items: when an issue is opened → add to To Do, when a PR is merged → move to Done. Use GitHub Actions with the project API for complex automation. Built-in workflows handle common patterns without code.

# Built-in project automations
Issue opened → add to project
Pull request merged → move to Done
Item reopened → move back to In Progress

details: GitHub Projects v2 is built on a flexible data model: items (issues/PRs/draft issues) have fields (status, priority, iteration, custom). Views are saved filter/sort/group configurations. The GraphQL API (ProjectV2 and ProjectV2Item nodes) supports CRUD on all project data. Wikis are a separate Git repo under repo.wiki.git; the default branch is master. Wiki revisions are preserved — any edit can be reverted. Discussions store threads in GitHub's database (not Git) with full-text search. Discussions support LaTeX via $$, Mermaid diagrams, and GitHub-flavored Markdown. Moderation features: reported content, hidden comments, and interaction limits. Projects can be organization-level or repository-level.
cheatSheet:
Core Commands

  • gh project create --org my-org --title "Sprint 24" — create project from CLI.
  • git clone https://github.com/user/repo.wiki.git — clone the wiki as a separate repo.
  • repo/discussions — URL for discussions.

Staff+ Patterns

  • Use project automation rules to reduce manual triage: auto-add issues to backlog, auto-move to Done on PR merge. This eliminates the "board drift" problem.
  • Wiki content should be version-controlled separately — the wiki repo has its own history, enabling rollback of documentation changes.

Watch Outs

  • Wiki repos default branch is master, not main. This can confuse automation scripts.
  • Discussion answers marked as "solution" are not automatically linked to issues. Use a manual process or Action to convert solutions into issues.

Interview Angle

Less common in interviews, but Uber/Meta ask about project management at scale: how do you track 2000 microservices across 50 teams? The answer is a unified project board per team with automated cross-referencing, not a single board for everything.
